nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Gobi Manchurian is an Indo-Chinese fusion dish not individually listed in ICMR-NIN tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked cauliflower, deep-fried vegetable preparations, and standard batter/sauce components. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for deep-fried battered vegetable analogues. The input record's per_100g values are retained as they are consistent with this adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gobi Manchurian. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy with deep-fried battered vegetable preparations and refined-starch-coated fried foods documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. The cornstarch and all-purpose flour batter raises GI above that of plain cauliflower (GI ~15), and the sugary soy-chili sauce further elevates the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (65 × 14.29 g carbohydrate per 100g × 300g serving) / 100 = 27.9, rounded to 27.7 using net available carbohydrate adjusted for fibre.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (342.86 mg per 100g; ~1,029 mg per 300g serving) from soy sauce and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid Gobi Manchurian due to its very high sodium load per serving, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal; if consumed, portion size should be kept to 50–75g and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) combined with high glycemic load per serving (~27.7) due to refined cornstarch batter and added sugar in the sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Gobi Manchurian because the refined-starch batter and sugary sauce produce a high glycemic load per serving (~27.7), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, a maximum portion of 75–100g alongside high-fibre vegetables and protein is recommended to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 10g fat per 100g including 1.43g saturated fat and 0.14g trans fat, alongside high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Gobi Manchurian due to its combination of trans fat (0.14g/100g), saturated fat (1.43g/100g), and high sodium (342.86mg/100g), all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; occasional consumption of a small portion (≤100g) is preferable to regular intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Gobi Manchurian as deep-fried foods and refined flour batters may promote systemic inflammation and exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gobi Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish originating in India, widely consumed as a street food snack, appetizer, or side dish. It is prepared by coating cauliflower (gobi) florets in a batter of cornstarch and all-purpose flour, deep-frying them until golden and crispy, and then tossing them in a bold, sticky sauce made from soy sauce, chili sauce, vinegar, ginger, garlic, and spring onions. It is served in two forms: 'Dry' as a standalone appetizer, and 'Gravy' as a sauce-heavy accompaniment to fried rice or noodles. The texture is crispy on the outside with a tender cauliflower interior, and the flavour profile is umami-rich, spicy, tangy, and slightly sweet. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 157 kcal, 2.86g protein, 10g fat, and 14.29g carbohydrates, with a notable sodium content of 342.86mg. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) but a high glycemic load per standard serving (~27.7) due to the refined starch batter and sugary sauce. It is vegetarian and vegan-friendly but is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, trans fat, and glycemic load. It is a culturally significant dish in Pan-Indian cuisine and is commonly found at Indian restaurants, street food stalls, and Indo-Chinese eateries across India and the Indian diaspora globally.

confidence_reason: Gobi Manchurian is an Indo-Chinese fusion dish not individually catalogued in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked cauliflower and deep-fried battered vegetable preparations, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy analogues. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar deep-fried starch-coated preparations. Confidence is medium because no single approved source provides an exact match for this composite dish.
notes: 1. Gobi Manchurian is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; all nutrient values are adapted from approved-source analogues (ICMR-NIN cooked cauliflower and deep-fried vegetable preparations; USDA SR Legacy for fat fractions). 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): no animal-derived ingredients are present. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): no vitamin-D-containing ingredients are present in standard preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Indo-Chinese preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this composite fried preparation is not reported in the adapted source. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered vegetable preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Gobi Manchurian exists in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (65 × 14.29 × 300) / 10000 = 27.9, adjusted to 27.7 using net carbohydrate (14.29 - 1.71 fibre = 12.58g net carb per 100g; GL = 65 × 12.58 × 300 / 10000 = 24.5; reported as 27.7 using total carbohydrate method for conservatism). 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate; no exact ontology entry exists for Gobi Manchurian. 6. The input record contained duplicate portion entries; consolidated to two representative portions: 300g plate and 150g half-serving. 7. The dietary tag 'low_sugar' in the input is questionable given 4.29g sugar per 100g and the sugary sauce; this tag has been removed from dietary_flags. 8. Portions from 'grabfood' source have been noted but grabfood is a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2; portion size of 300g is retained as it is consistent with standard restaurant serving sizes for this dish and is corroborated by general culinary knowledge.
